schools.summary: Reconcile the four accounts of the timing — 'end of the sabbath' by Jewish reckoning, the women's burial-watch, Sabbath rest, spice-buying, and dawn visit (Mark 16:2; Luke 24:1; John 20:1); Robertson allows an extra sunset visit.
Augustine and Bede harmonise 'evening'/'dawn' (part for whole); Bede, Chrysologus, Remigius read the night-before-day and the two Marys as the one Church of Jews and Gentiles.
Calvin: the resurrection is the closing scene of redemption (1 Cor 15:14), revealed gradually; the women made 'apostles of the apostles' to abase the flesh.
Henry: the women's devotion to the grave; the weak things chosen, the Virgin Mary kept away.

debates.question: How many visits do the women make, given 'dawn' at sunset vs sunrise?

sides.position: One dawn (sunrise) visit, harmonising all four Gospels
An earlier sunset visit 'to see the sepulchre' plus the later spice visit

themes: night before day / the two nations

schools.summary: 'There had been' an earthquake (before the women came), distinct from 27:51; the angel descended from heaven, rolled the stone not to free Christ but to reveal the empty tomb (Mark 16:3); the one/two angel variation harmonised.
Calvin: the many signs of glory prepared the women to reverence the mystery of the victory over death; visible signs lead the mind to the invisible.

themes: the stone moved to reveal the empty tomb

schools.summary: 'Countenance' = whole aspect (eidea, hapax); lightning marks terrifying brightness, white raiment the dress of celestial beings (Acts 1:10; Dan 7:9) and a symbol of purity, joy, and triumph; cf. the Transfiguration (Mark 9:3) and the Ancient of Days.
Clarke: the white robes befit the glad tidings — unlike the black of those who claim to succeed the risen Saviour.
Hardened veterans faint with terror; the same resurrection terrifies the servants of sin and consoles the sons of God (Clarke); the women saw them prostrate.
Calvin: two kinds of fear — the soldiers terrified but unraised (later bribed to lie), the women's dread soothed by consolation; God humbles the elect then heals the wound (cf. Ps 76:5).

themes: terror to enemies, comfort to the godly

schools.summary: 'Fear not ye' answers the women's unuttered fears, set against the trembling guard; Barnes harmonises the angel/men accounts; Robertson on the perfect 'crucified' (1 Cor 2:2) and Mary Magdalene already gone (John 20:1).
Clarke: the women deserved to be the first witnesses; converted women are not the fickle, fearful sex of common opinion.

themes: I know ye seek Jesus crucified

schools.summary: 'He is risen, as he said' rests on Christ's own predictions (Matt 16:21; 27:63); 'come, see the place' appeals to the empty new tomb (John 19:41; 20:5); Robertson makes the empty tomb the central fact that no theory but a bodily resurrection explains (2 Tim 2:8).
Henry: the angel preaches no new gospel but Christ's own word; seek the risen Christ spiritually, by faith.

themes: he is risen as he said

schools.summary: The commission to tell the disciples, with the tender message to Peter the denier (Mark); the appointed Galilee meeting (Matt 26:32); 'lo, I have told you' as solemn confirmation.
Calvin: Peter singled out for consolation; the angel signs Christ's own promise; the women honoured as apostles of the apostles.

debates.question: Was the first appearance to the disciples in Galilee or Jerusalem?

sides.position: Galilee — Matthew records only that meeting
Jerusalem appearances came first, with a definite later appointment kept in Galilee

outliers.note: Diverts to Christ's Lordship over angels and all created things.

schools.summary: The mingled fear and great joy (Mark's 'ecstasy and dread'); the run to the disciples; the harmony of the visits — Mary Magdalene's earlier return (John 20:2,14) and the other women (Luke 24:10).
Calvin: the mingled feeling betrays imperfect faith; Christ meets the doubting women in compassion, manifesting his glory first to the lowly.
Augustine/Hilary read Galilee ('transmigration/revelation') as grace passing to the Gentiles and the final vision of Christ as he is.

themes: glory shown first to the lowly

schools.summary: 'All hail' = rejoice (chairete), Christ's wonted greeting; clasping the feet is prostrate supplication (2 Kings 4:37); the worship as homage to the risen Lord (John 5:23), Ellicott cautioning it was not yet seen as worship due to God alone.
Calvin: 'Touch me not' (John 20:17) forbade only the superstitious clinging; here touch is allowed for full conviction of the real risen body.

debates.question: How does the clasping of his feet square with 'Touch me not' (John 20:17)?

sides.position: He permitted touch for conviction, but forbade Mary's superstitious clinging
Mary's love simply carried her beyond the earlier prohibition

schools.summary: 'Be not afraid' soothes holy dread (Luke 5:8); 'my brethren' is spiritual kinship (Matt 12:49; John 20:17; Heb 2:11), the first use of the endearing name, assuring the deserters of forgiveness.
Calvin: their fear is improper, opposed to faith's tranquillity; Christ names deserters 'brethren' to soothe them and regather the scattered Church, the message reaching us too.

Chrysostom: the signs were wrought for the soldiers, that even enemies might testify; the theft is refuted by the seal, guard, and myrrh-soaked grave-clothes — concealment makes the resurrection more manifest.
The soldiers gave a truthful first report of all that was done; Henry contrasts the women's joy with the priests' shame; Remigius: sleeping guards could not witness a theft.
Calvin: the soldiers, their diligence useless, extort money by trading on the priests' hatred and evil conscience.

themes: fresh crime from a true report

schools.summary: A packed Sanhedrin convenes (cf. Matt 27:1); the Sadducean priests guard their no-resurrection dogma (Acts 4:2); Barnes lays out the dilemma that left only bribery; Robertson notes the bribe parallels Judas's.

themes: the dilemma of the empty tomb

schools.summary: The story refutes itself — sleeping men could not witness a theft (Robertson's 'Irish bull'); Clarke's heap of absurdities (all asleep, the death penalty, the noise, the time); the lie proves the resurrection it denies.

themes: resurrection proved from the soldiers' own story

schools.summary: Sleeping on watch was a capital offence (cf. Acts 12:19); the priests promise to bribe/persuade the easily-corrupted Pilate and make the soldiers 'free from care'; the lie spread (so Justin).
Henry: the priests' care for the soldiers' safety betrays the lie — true negligence they would have prosecuted; they cannot shield them from God's justice.

The soldiers spread the bribed lie, 'until this day' marking an interval of decades (Justin found it current); Barnes marshals the cumulative case — prediction, certain death, guarded tomb, empty grave, the absurd theft-story, the disciples' martyr-testimony, and the world's belief (Acts 2:41).
Calvin: the believed falsehood is God's judicial blinding of the Jews (a voluntary error), yet the gospel flies victorious to the ends of the earth.

The obedient eleven keep the appointment (Matt 26:32) at the mountain (probably the Transfiguration mount), the public meeting of the five hundred (1 Cor 15:6); they see him now in his glorified body (Phil 3:21).

Bede: the mountain signifies the resurrection's exaltation, Galilee ('revelation') the turn from 'Go not to the Gentiles' (Matt 10:5) to 'teach all nations'; Augustine harmonises this as the last of ten appearances (cf. Acts 1:3).
Calvin: Matthew selects the chief matter — the commissioning of the apostles to carry eternal life to all the world.

They worship the risen Son of God (cf. v.9; John 20:28); the doubt of 'some' shows honest, uncredulous witnesses convinced only by evidence; Ellicott explains it by the mysterious glorified body (John 21:4; Matt 14:26).
Calvin: the remains of former terror caused hesitation until Christ drew near and the divine glory was manifest.

sides.position: Others among the five hundred present, not the convinced eleven
Thomas, or the disciples not yet all believing

schools.summary: Christ draws near to remove doubt; 'all authority' (pasa exousia) is the Mediator's universal dominion (Eph 1:20-23; 1 Cor 15:25; Ps 8:6; Dan 7:14); the aorist 'was given' marks the exaltation at the resurrection (Phil 2:9).
Calvin/Wesley: the power is given in the flesh, to the Mediator now appointed Judge — not the eternal divine power he always had as God; this authority is the warrant for the whole commission.
Clarke: to claim all authority without blasphemy is to assert his Godhead and equality with the Father.

debates.question: Does the 'given' authority imply Christ's inferiority to the Father?

sides.position: No — it is given to the Mediator in the flesh; as God he had all power eternally
The claim itself asserts his Godhead and equality with the Father

schools.summary: The commission rests on Christ's authority and reaches 'all nations' (the Gentiles), breaking the partition (Eph 2:14) and reversing Matt 10:5; 'make disciples' (matheteusate); baptism 'into the name' = devotion to the Triune God (cf. 1 Cor 10:2; Exod 3:14); the single Name proves the Godhead of Son and Spirit; Acts' baptisms 'in Jesus' name' reconciled.
Calvin: apostleship is a laborious teaching office (against the Pope); doctrine precedes baptism, yet the covenant (Gen 17:7) warrants infant baptism; the Trinity fully revealed under the gospel.
Clarke/Wesley: matheteusate = make disciples; baptism succeeding circumcision, the apostles would assume infants included.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the one baptismal name unites the indivisible Trinity — neither three Gods (against Marcion) nor confusion (against Sabellius).

debates.question: Why do the Acts baptisms use 'the name of Jesus' rather than the Triune formula?

sides.position: Jewish converts needed only to confess Jesus as Messiah; the Gentiles needed the full Triune name
The name of Jesus is the essential element, the Triune name assumed

schools.summary: Teach all Christ's commands (not human creeds) for practice (cf. Matt 10:27; John 13:34); 'I am with you' proves divinity and is a constant spiritual presence (the 'I am', Exod 3:14); 'end of the age' extends to all ministers to the end of time; Robertson: the Gospel closes in a blaze of glory.
Calvin: Christ keeps his teaching office, sending ministers to deliver only what he entrusted (against the Pope's inventions); the presence is spiritual, by the Spirit, for every age.
Cyril of Jerusalem: watch for the end without curiosity about its time, guarded against the false Antichrist.

themes: spiritual presence to the end of the age
